en_body:
(A message to all honored Cowherd Boys: please do not be so lost in love that you
 let the buffalo graze while neglecting military/official duties: lest you be punished
and parted from your honored Weaver Maidens)
...................................................Hoàng-Ân........................

"Buffalo! I tell you this,
 Come to the field, buffalo, plow with me.
Transplanting and plowing are the farmer's work,
 You plow, I plant—who would count the labor?
Tomorrow the rice will ripen over the fields,
 Worth all our plowing and planting through sun and rain."
 (Vietnamese Folk-Song)

The Weaver Maiden, graceful and tender,
Lovely, weaving silk beside the Tương River.
The Cowherd tends buffalo in the neighboring fields,
Enchanted by the maiden's beautiful hands.
Who knew they would neglect the Royal work:
Not enough silk to clothe the peach-gathering Immortals.
And he—not enough Celestial wine,
The buffalo left untended, devouring golden rice.

Punished, the Lover/Maiden must be divided,
Once a year, in the Seventh Month, pitying crows bridge the sky.
.............................................................................................

KOOL BUFFALO
note: The poem playfully retells the Cowherd and Weaver Maiden legend, whose lovers meet once yearly across a bridge of birds in the seventh lunar month.
